fuzzy_prd:
We need a report generator that turns structured test-run data into a small set of browsable HTML pages for developers and CI dashboards. The input is a set of test class records, each containing the class name, overall counts (total tests run, errors, failures, skipped), and a list of individual test cases with their name, duration, and outcome text. The generator should produce two kinds of pages: a summary index that lists every class in a table with its counts and a hyperlink to that class's detail page, and a per-class detail page that lists every individual test case with duration and outcome. Both pages should use a shared visual structure — a standard HTML5 document with a linked stylesheet — and both should pretty-print the markup with two-space indentation for readability. Lines must end with carriage-return-plus-linefeed so the output is consistent across operating systems. Rows in both tables should carry a style hint so a stylesheet can color-code them: a row where everything passed gets tagged as successful, a row with at least one failure gets tagged as failed, and a row with errors or skips but no failures gets no tag at all. The detail page also includes a back link to the summary. The stylesheet reference should incorporate the configured output directory path. Count fields that cannot be parsed as integers should silently fall back to zero rather than causing an error. When the class list is empty the table still appears, just with only the header row.

One quick follow-up from the questions that came in: yes, all lines in the generated HTML output are terminated with CRLF (carriage return + line feed, \r\n), and that applies to both the summary index page and every class detail page, not just one of them.

Also on the row styling, the intent is very literal. A row where errors, failures, and skipped are all zero gets class="success". A row with at least one failure, regardless of any other counts, gets class="failure" — failure takes precedence over everything else. If there are no failures but there is at least one error or skip, then the row carries NO class attribute at all, meaning no class= on the tr element.

For links and shared page structure, in the summary table each class name should link to './classes/<name>.html'. On the detail page, the Home link href should be the verbatim 'homepage' string passed as input. The stylesheet href should be output_dir + '/css/style.css', joined with a forward slash. And both pages should use the exact same basic shell: <!DOCTYPE html>, <html>, <head> with a single <link rel="stylesheet" href="{outputDir}/css/style.css" />, and <body>. No other meta tags, no script tags, and no XML declaration.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: fun renderSummary(outputDir: String, classes: List<TestClassSummary>): String; fun renderClassDetail(outputDir: String, homepage: String, testClass: TestClassDetail): String

trigger_keywords: what is the exact indentation of the HTML
how many spaces per indent level
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The HTML is pretty-printed with exactly 2-space indentation per nesting level. The document starts with '<!DOCTYPE html>' on its own line, then '<html>' with no indentation, then '<head>' and '<body>' at 2-space indent, and so on recursively.

trigger_keywords: what CSS class for failed test case row
how is a non-Passed result marked in detail page
detail page row style for Skipped or Failed result
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: In the class detail page, a test case row gets class="success" if and only if the result text is exactly the string 'Passed' (case-sensitive). Any other result value — 'Failed', 'Skipped', 'Error', or anything else — gets class="failure".

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: a back link to the summary
context_pointers.actual_meaning:
In the class detail page, immediately after the

Class

heading, there is an Home element, where homepage is the string passed in the request's 'homepage' field.

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific information about that constraint. Please refer to the project PRD and the golden implementation under kotest__kotest/golden/ for the authoritative specification.
